In [1]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
# Load the data
data = pd.read_csv('Data-Week4.csv') 
# List of numerical attributes
numerical_attributes = ['Total', 'HP', 'Attack', 'Defense', 'Sp. Atk', 'Sp. Def', 'Speed']
# Create subplots for visualizations
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=3, ncols=3, figsize=(15, 12))
fig.suptitle('Histograms of Numerical Attributes', fontsize=16)

for i, attribute in enumerate(numerical_attributes):
    row, col = i // 3, i % 3
    ax = axes[row, col]

    # Histogram
    sns.histplot(data=data, x=attribute, kde=True, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f'Histogram of {attribute}')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.subplots_adjust(top=0.9)
In [2]:
# Create density plots
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))
plt.suptitle('Density Plots of Numerical Attributes', fontsize=16)

for i, attribute in enumerate(numerical_attributes):
    plt.subplot(1, len(numerical_attributes), i + 1)
    sns.kdeplot(data=data, x=attribute, fill=True)
    plt.title(f'Density Plot of {attribute}')

plt.tight_layout()
In [3]:
# Create box plots
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.title('Box Plots of Numerical Attributes', fontsize=16)
sns.boxplot(data=data[numerical_attributes], orient='h')
plt.xlabel('Value')
plt.show()

The histogram shows the distribution of the total stats of Pokemon. For example if we take total attribute it appears that most Pokemon having total stats around 500.

The density plot provides a smoother representation of the distribution, confirming that the majority of Pokemon have total stats concentrated in the 500 range.

The box plot is useful for understanding the spread and presence of outliers in the data.The "box" in the box plot represents the interquartile range (IQR), which contains the middle 50% of the data.

In [4]:
# Import necessary libraries
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Select the numerical attributes you want to include
numerical_attributes = ['Total', 'HP', 'Attack', 'Defense', 'Sp. Atk', 'Sp. Def', 'Speed']

# Extract the subset of the dataset containing only these attributes
numerical_data = data[numerical_attributes]


# Calculate the correlation matrix for the selected attributes
correlation_matrix = numerical_data.corr()

# Create a heatmap to visualize the correlation matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Pokemon Attributes')
plt.show()
In [5]:
# Scatter Plots (pairwise)
sns.scatterplot(x='Attack', y='Defense', data= data , hue='Legendary', palette='Set1')
plt.title('Scatter Plot of Pokemon Attack vs. Defense')
plt.show()
sns.scatterplot(x='Sp. Atk', y='Sp. Def', data=data, hue='Legendary', palette='Set1')
plt.title('Scatter Plot of Pokemon Sp. Atk vs Sp. Def')
plt.show()
sns.scatterplot(x='Speed', y='HP', data=data, hue='Legendary', palette='Set1')
plt.title('Scatter Plot of Pokemon Speed vs  HP')
plt.show()

In feature selection, it's essential to consider which features are relevant for our analysis. The choice of features can depend on specific objectives. Here are some considerations:

For the correlation heatmap, we can identify features that are highly correlated with the 'Total' attribute. Features with strong positive or negative correlations may be important for predicting a Pokemon's overall strength.

For scatter plots, you can visualize relationships between pairs of features. For example, 'Attack' vs. 'Defense' is a common pair to consider, we can see here a moderate positive linear relationship between Attack and Defense attributes. If we consider 'Sp. Atk vs Sp. Def' thier is extremely strong positive linear relationship between the two attributes. If we consider 'Speed vs HP' their is relatively weak positive linear relationship between two attributes. We can also use color (hue) to differentiate between Legendary and non-Legendary Pokemon to see if there are any distinguishing patterns.
